content:
# 1 Peter Chapter 3 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 267 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1PE_003_page_001.jpg

1. Likewise, wives, be subject to your own husbands, so that even if some disobey the word, they may be won without a word through the conduct of their wives,

2. when they observe your pure conduct in fear.

3. Let your adorning not be the outward adorning of braiding of hair and putting on of gold jewelry or wearing of garments,

4. but let it be the hidden person of the heart, in the imperishable quality of a gentle and quiet spirit, which is of great worth before God.

5. For in this manner formerly the holy women also, who hoped in God, adorned themselves, being subject to their own husbands,

6. as Sarah obeyed Abraham, calling him lord. You have become her children if you do good and do not fear any intimidation.

7. Likewise, husbands, live together according to knowledge, as with a weaker vessel — the female — showing honor as also being fellow heirs of the grace of life, so that your prayers may not be hindered.

8. Finally, all of you be of one mind, sympathetic, loving the brothers, compassionate, humble-minded,

9. not returning evil for evil, or reviling for reviling, but on the contrary blessing, because to this you were called, that you might inherit a blessing.

10. For "the one who desires to love life and to see good days, let him restrain his tongue from evil, and his lips from speaking deceit;

11. let him turn away from evil and do good; let him seek peace and pursue it.

12. For the eyes of the Lord are upon the righteous, and his ears are open to their prayer; but the face of the Lord is against those who do evil."

13. And who is there to harm you if you become zealous for what is good?

14. But even if you should suffer for the sake of righteousness, you are blessed. And do not fear their fear, nor be troubled,

15. but sanctify the Lord Christ in your hearts, always being prepared to make a defense to everyone who asks you for a reason concerning the hope that is in you, yet with gentleness and fear,

16. having a good conscience, so that in the matter in which you are spoken against, those who revile your good conduct in Christ may be put to shame.

17. For it is better to suffer for doing good, if the will of God should so will it, than for doing evil.

18. Because Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, that he might bring you to God, being put to death in the flesh but made alive in the spirit,

19. in which also he went and proclaimed to the spirits in prison,

20. who were formerly disobedient, when the patience of God waited in the days of Noah, while the ark was being prepared, in which a few — that is, eight souls — were saved through water.

21. Which also, as an antitype, now saves you — baptism — not the putting away of the filth of the flesh, but the appeal of a good conscience toward God, through the resurrection of Jesus Christ,

22. who has gone into heaven and is at the right hand of God, with angels and authorities and powers having been subjected to him.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **"Be subject" (v.1) — ὑποτασσόμεναι:** The participle continues the household code structure begun in 2:13-18. The Sinaiticus reading preserves the standard participial form. The word does not connote servility but voluntary ordering within a social structure, parallel to the submission to governing authorities (2:13) and of slaves to masters (2:18). The evangelistic motive — "won without a word" (ἄνευ λόγου) — is notable: conduct, not argument, is the persuasive instrument.

2. **"Braiding of hair and putting on of gold" (v.3):** ἐμπλοκῆς τριχῶν καὶ περιθέσεως χρυσίων. The Sinaiticus text reads with the standard tradition here. This is not an absolute prohibition of adornment but a contrast: external versus internal beauty. The triad (hair, gold, garments) mirrors similar critiques in Isaiah 3:18-24 and 1 Timothy 2:9.

3. **"The hidden person of the heart" (v.4) — ὁ κρυπτὸς τῆς καρδίας ἄνθρωπος:** A striking phrase found only here in the New Testament. The "imperishable" (ἀφθάρτου) quality of the inner adornment contrasts with the perishable gold of v.3, echoing the "imperishable" inheritance of 1:4 and the "imperishable seed" of 1:23.

4. **"Sanctify the Lord Christ" (v.15) — κύριον δὲ τὸν Χριστὸν ἁγιάσατε:** Sinaiticus reads "Christ" (Χριστόν) where later manuscripts read "God" (θεόν). This is a significant textual variant. The passage alludes to Isaiah 8:13 LXX ("sanctify the Lord himself"), applying to Christ a text originally about YHWH — a high Christological claim. The defense (ἀπολογία) language may reflect formal legal proceedings, but the context suggests any situation where believers must explain their hope.

5. **"Suffered once for sins" (v.18) — Χριστὸς ἅπαξ περὶ ἁμαρτιῶν ἔπαθεν:** Sinaiticus reads ἔπαθεν ("suffered") rather than ἀπέθανεν ("died"), a characteristic Petrine emphasis on Christ's suffering (cf. 2:21, 4:1). The phrase "righteous for the unrighteous" (δίκαιος ὑπὲρ ἀδίκων) is a compressed statement of substitutionary atonement. "That he might bring you to God" (ἵνα ὑμᾶς προσαγάγῃ τῷ θεῷ) — Sinaiticus reads "you" (ὑμᾶς) where some manuscripts read "us" (ἡμᾶς).

6. **"Spirits in prison" (vv.19-20):** One of the most debated passages in the New Testament. Three major interpretations: (a) Christ's pre-existent spirit preaching through Noah; (b) Christ's descent to Hades between death and resurrection; (c) Christ's proclamation to fallen angels (cf. 1 Enoch 6-16) during his ascension. The "spirits" (πνεύμασιν) more naturally refers to supernatural beings than to human souls, and the connection to Noah's generation aligns with the Enochic tradition of imprisoned fallen angels. The formal structure links this proclamation to the triumph declared in v.22.

7. **"Baptism — as an antitype" (v.21) — ἀντίτυπον νῦν σῴζει βάπτισμα:** The flood waters are the type; baptism is the antitype. Peter immediately clarifies that baptism's saving power is not in physical cleansing ("not the putting away of the filth of the flesh") but in the "appeal" (ἐπερώτημα) — or possibly "pledge" — of a good conscience toward God. The word ἐπερώτημα is rare and debated: it can mean "inquiry," "appeal," or "pledge/commitment."

8. **"Angels and authorities and powers subjected to him" (v.22):** The chapter ends with a cosmic Christological climax. The heavenly session at God's right hand (Psalm 110:1) and subjection of spiritual powers completes the arc begun in v.18: Christ's suffering leads through death, resurrection, and ascension to universal sovereignty. This frames the entire chapter's ethical instruction — believers can endure suffering because the one who suffered for them now reigns over all powers.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
